items.html:
The wider framework

Information governance is the way an organisation looks after the information it holds — making sure it is handled legally, securely and ethically, and used to support good care. It is far wider than a single law. It brings together the UK GDPR and the Data Protection Act 2018, the common law duty of confidentiality, records management, and your employer’s own policies and procedures. Good information governance means there are clear rules for how information is collected, recorded, stored, shared and eventually destroyed, and that everyone is trained to follow them. For you as a care worker it turns abstract law into everyday habits: writing accurate notes, keeping records secure, sharing only what is needed, and knowing who to ask when you are unsure.

Roles and the Data Security & Protection Toolkit

Several roles support information governance in health and social care.

  • Data Protection Officer (DPO): advises the organisation on data protection law and is the contact point for the Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO).
  • Caldicott Guardian: a senior person who protects the confidentiality of people’s health and care information and makes sure it is shared appropriately.
  • Senior Information Risk Owner (SIRO): owns information risk at a senior level.

Organisations that handle NHS patient data also complete the Data Security and Protection Toolkit each year — an online self-assessment against the national data security standards. You do not run these systems yourself, but knowing they exist tells you where to turn: your manager, your policies, and ultimately your DPO or Caldicott Guardian when a difficult question arises.

items.question: You are unsure whether some sensitive health information about a person you support may be shared with another agency. Who is the senior person specifically responsible for protecting confidentiality and enabling appropriate sharing of health and care information?

answers.text: The Caldicott Guardian, whose role is to protect the confidentiality of health and care information and make sure it is shared appropriately.
answers.feedback: Correct. The Caldicott Guardian exists precisely to balance protecting confidentiality with sharing information when it is right to do so.

answers.text: The Data Protection Officer, whose only job is to keep all records locked away and never shared.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. The DPO advises on the law and is the ICO contact point, but does not exist simply to block all sharing.

answers.text: The Information Commissioner, who personally approves every sharing decision in each care home.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. The ICO is the regulator; it does not sign off individual day-to-day sharing decisions.

answers.text: There is no such role; a decision like this is always left to whoever happens to be on shift.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. There is a dedicated role, the Caldicott Guardian, and difficult decisions should be escalated, not guessed at.

items.html:
Having a lawful basis

Under the UK GDPR you may only use personal information if you have a lawful basis for doing so. There are six lawful bases, including consent, legal obligation, vital interests, public task and legitimate interests. In health and social care, day-to-day record-keeping usually does not rely on the person’s consent. Instead it rests on bases such as a legal obligation, a public task, or the organisation’s legitimate interests in providing safe care. This matters because consent can be withdrawn at any time — and care records still need to be kept accurately whether or not someone consents. Consent to your care or treatment is a separate, important idea from the lawful basis for processing the data.

Special category data and valid consent

Health information is special category data, alongside details such as race, religion, sexual orientation and genetic data. Because it is more sensitive, using it needs an extra safeguard: a specific Article 9 condition on top of your lawful basis — commonly the condition for providing health or social care. When consent is genuinely the basis, the UK GDPR sets a high bar. Valid consent must be freely given, specific, informed and unambiguous, shown by a clear positive action — never assumed from silence, inactivity or a pre-ticked box. It can also be withdrawn as easily as it was given. Understanding this stops you relying on ‘consent’ when a firmer, more appropriate basis actually applies.

items.question: A care organisation records health information in order to provide care. Which statement best reflects the UK GDPR position on the lawful basis for this?

answers.text: It usually relies on a basis such as legal obligation, public task or legitimate interests, plus a special category condition for the health data — not on the person’s consent.
answers.feedback: Correct. Care records typically rest on a firmer basis than consent, with an additional Article 9 condition for the sensitive health information.

answers.text: Consent is always the only valid basis, so care cannot be recorded unless the person ticks a consent box.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Consent is one of six bases and is often not the right one for care records, which must be kept accurately regardless.

answers.text: Because the information is sensitive, no lawful basis is needed at all.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Sensitive data needs more protection, not less — a lawful basis and an extra special category condition are both required.

answers.text: A single lawful basis covers everything, so special category health data needs no additional safeguard.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Special category data such as health always needs an additional Article 9 condition on top of the lawful basis.

items.html:
The Caldicott principles

The Caldicott principles guide how confidential health and care information should be handled. They ask you to justify the purpose of every use of that information, and not to use it unless it is genuinely necessary. When you do use it, you should use the minimum amount required for the task — no more ‘just in case’. Access should be on a strict need-to-know basis, so that people see only the information their role requires. Everyone handling confidential information must understand their responsibilities and comply with the law. A later principle also asks organisations to be open with people about how their information is used, so there are no surprises.

Need to know, and the duty to share

‘Need to know’ is a practical test: before you look at, discuss or pass on information, ask whether you actually need it to do your job or keep someone safe. Curiosity is never a good enough reason. At the same time, protecting information is not the same as hoarding it. One Caldicott principle makes clear that the duty to share information can be as important as the duty to protect it. If a person could be harmed because professionals held back information that should have been shared, that failure is just as serious as an inappropriate disclosure. Safe, joined-up care depends on getting this balance right, and on recording your reasons.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the summary of the Caldicott principles.
items.textField: You should use the *minimum* amount of confidential information needed, and only access records on a strict need-to-*know* basis. Every use of that information must have a justified *purpose*. One principle reminds us that the duty to *share* can be as important as the duty to protect, so that people receive safe, joined-up *care*.

items.html:
Passwords, devices and phishing

Most information now lives on computers, phones and care systems, so everyday digital habits are part of keeping it safe. Use a strong, unique password or passphrase for each account, never share your login, and switch on multi-factor authentication where it is offered. Lock your screen whenever you step away, and never leave a device unattended where others could use it. Be alert to phishing: emails, texts or calls that try to trick you into clicking a link, opening an attachment or handing over details. Check who a message is really from, do not be rushed by urgent-sounding demands, and if something feels wrong, report it rather than clicking.

Remote working and social media

Working away from the office brings extra care. Only use devices and networks your employer allows, keep records out of sight of family and members of the public, and avoid working over unsecured public Wi-Fi. Do not save confidential information onto personal phones, laptops or memory sticks unless your policy permits it and it is properly encrypted. Social media deserves special caution: never post anything that identifies the people you support, share photographs of them or their homes, or discuss your work in a way that could reveal who is being cared for. Your duty of confidentiality does not clock off when your shift ends — it applies online, at home and among friends.

items.question: It is fine to share your care-system login with a colleague who has forgotten theirs, as long as you trust them and it saves time.
items.correctMsg: Correct — this is false. Logins must never be shared, even with someone you trust: they identify who did what, and sharing them breaks that accountability and your security policy. Your colleague should get their own access sorted out.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite — this is false. Sharing logins is never acceptable, because records must show exactly who accessed or changed information; a forgotten password should be reset, not worked around.

items.html:
Subject access requests and records rights

People have a legal right to see the personal information held about them. When someone asks for a copy of their own records, this is a subject access request. They are entitled to a copy, normally free of charge, and the organisation must respond without undue delay — usually within one month. You are not expected to handle a request alone or hand over a file on the spot. Your job is to recognise the request, treat it seriously, and pass it promptly to your manager or the responsible person, who will confirm the requester’s identity and make sure information about other people is properly protected before anything is released.

Keeping records only as long as needed

Information should be kept accurate and up to date, and held only for as long as there is a genuine reason to keep it. Organisations follow retention schedules — for health and care records these are set out in the Records Management Code of Practice — which say how long different records should be kept. Keeping information forever ‘just in case’ is not lawful, and neither is destroying records too early. When information does reach the end of its life, it must be disposed of securely: paper shredded or placed in confidential waste, and electronic records deleted so they cannot be recovered. Never simply bin confidential paperwork or leave old devices lying around.

items.question: A person you support says: ‘I want to see everything that is written about me in my care file.’ What is the correct response?

answers.text: Treat it as a subject access request: they have a right to a copy of their information, so pass the request promptly to your manager or the responsible person to handle, usually within one month.
answers.feedback: Correct. This is a subject access request; recognising it and escalating it properly is exactly what is expected of you.

answers.text: Refuse, because care records belong to the organisation and people have no right to see what is written about them.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. People have a clear legal right of access to their own personal information; refusing outright is unlawful.

answers.text: Hand over the whole file on the spot yourself, including notes about other people, without telling anyone.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Requests must be handled properly, with identity checked and other people’s information protected before anything is released.

answers.text: Tell them they must pay a large fee and wait at least a year for a decision.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. A copy is normally provided free of charge and the response is usually due within one month, not a year.
